chunk_summary: This section outlines developmental feeding skills and oral-motor milestones, emphasizing age-specific texture progression, self-feeding, and readiness for complementary feeding, with considerations for preterm infants and cultural feeding practices in India.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific oral-motor milestones for feeding skill development.
Identify readiness indicators for complementary feeding in infants, including preterm considerations.
Recognize feeding challenges and referral criteria for SLP or OT intervention.
Learn cultural influences on feeding practices and their impact on skill progression.
key_facts: Complementary feeding readiness typically begins at 6 months with sitting support, reduced tongue-thrust, and spoon-fed puree management.
From 6–8 months, infants progress to thick purees and mashed foods; by 8–10 months, they manage soft lumps, finger foods, and cup sipping.
Delaying lump textures beyond 8–10 months increases feeding refusal risk.
By 10–12 months, rotary chewing emerges, and infants tolerate chopped family foods and assisted cup drinking.
Prolonged bottle use or exclusive 'milk-and-biscuit' feeding patterns in India can mask feeding skill delays.
Referral to SLP or OT is warranted for persistent gagging, inability to progress beyond purees, prolonged meals, or recurrent respiratory symptoms during feeding.
reference_quote: Readiness for complementary feeding is usually present around 6 months when the infant can sit with support, brings hands to mouth, has reduced tongue-thrust, and can manage a spoon-fed puree. (ASHA developmental milestones)
document_key_topics: Developmental feeding skills and oral-motor milestones
Cultural feeding practices and their impact
